name: String Replace
description: Replace an exact string in a file — no line numbers needed, more reliable than patch for simple edits.

function.name: str_replace
function.description:
Replace an exact string occurrence in a file. Unlike patch, this tool does not require line numbers — it finds the literal text and replaces it. This makes it safe to use even after other edits have shifted line numbers. Use this for simple targeted edits; use patch only when inserting/deleting blocks of many lines at once.

IMPORTANT: Always read_file immediately before calling this tool so that old_str matches the current file content exactly. Include enough surrounding context in old_str to make the match unambiguous.

file_path.description: Absolute or relative path to the file to edit.

old_str.description: The exact string to find and replace. Must match the file content verbatim, including indentation and whitespace. Include 1-2 lines of surrounding context to avoid ambiguous matches.

new_str.description: The replacement string. Use an empty string to delete old_str.

count.description: Number of replacements to make (default 1). The file must contain exactly this many occurrences of old_str, otherwise an error is returned.

system_prompt:
## str_replace — Usage Rules

**Prefer str_replace over patch for simple edits.** It is more reliable because it does not depend on line numbers.

### When to use str_replace vs patch
- `str_replace`: replacing a function body, changing a value, fixing a line — anything where you can identify the text uniquely
- `patch`: inserting a new block of code with no unique surrounding text, or making many changes across a file in one shot

### Workflow
1. `read_file` the target file to get the current content
2. Copy the exact text you want to replace into `old_str` (including 1-2 lines of context)
3. Write the replacement in `new_str`
4. If the error says 'not found', re-read the file — the content may have changed
5. If the error says 'found N times', make `old_str` more specific

### Common mistakes to avoid
- Do **not** guess `old_str` from memory — always copy it from the file
- Do **not** use partial matches; include enough context to be unambiguous
